comments.author: @olivergroning6421
comments.text:
1'40" : Nearly 30 years ago, the research group where I was doing my PhD organized an international meeting, and I (as a junior PhD student) was put in charge of the social program for the accompanying persons. We took a boat trip from Montreux and also visited Chillon Castle. No lie… one of the ladies said, ‘What a beautiful castle, but why did they build it so close to the highway?’

I could say where she was from, but you might guess — and you would probably be right.

replies.author: @RemoteEarthJourneys
replies.text: 😂 That's a fantastic story. It perfectly shows how our brains assume modern things came first, when in reality the castle had been there for centuries before the highway ever existed. I can only imagine everyone's reaction when she said it!

comments.author: @glenngriffin1784
comments.text: What's not mentioned in this video is the major league drawbacks to living in Sweden that make most countries in the world a lot better place to live. Every single good thing mentioned in this video is not worth the long dark brutal winters that last months, the social freeze witch means having friends is almost impossible. The cost of living makes just having necessities a luxury (many people actually sleep in straw beds even in the most modern looking housing. Note the inside of the average home is never shown).  Housing shortages force the average person to share small living spaces with many people. Healthcare wait times create a very dangerous situation when one becomes ill shortening the life span of many.  Bureaucracy and society in general operate on strict and confining rules that make daily life lack enjoyment for most and creates an atmosphere of constant animosity.

replies.author: @merlinf2869

replies.author: @AiorosSagittarius
replies.text: Absolutely. Am Swiss too, and while there is indeed no perfect country, some are still better to live in than others. When I see what's going on elsewhere, especially right now in 2026, I definitively feel blessed to be living here (not born, my father is Swiss but my mother was German and I was actually born in München/Munich. I've been living in Switzerland since I was 3 or 4 years old tho). And while we too have our black sheep in politics, the country as a whole looks well after its people, or at least tries to (and definitively more than some other countries, especially some outside of Europe).

comments.author: @EpicEarthYTB
comments.text: Switzerland is truly a masterpiece of nature and culture. The perfect harmony between tradition and modern innovation is so inspiring!

replies.author: @RemoteEarthJourneys
replies.text: I completely agree. What fascinates me most is how Switzerland seems to embrace progress without losing its identity. Not many places manage that balance so well. What aspect impressed you more—the culture or the landscapes?

replies.author: @BestTravel2
replies.text: So glad you picked up on that! I really tried to capture that unique blend of nature, culture, tradition, and modern innovation in the video. Thanks for watching!

comments.author: @TheLastPlaces-c6y
comments.text: Switzerland—The fact that people simply wake up every morning with the Alps right outside their windows still leaves me utterly astounded.

replies.author: @RemoteEarthJourneys
replies.text: It's amazing, isn't it? For many of us, the Alps are a once-in-a-lifetime destination, while for others they're simply the view from the kitchen window. Which Swiss town would you love to wake up in most?

replies.author: @BestTravel2
replies.text: It truly is a breathtaking sight that never gets old. Waking up to those majestic peaks is an experience like no other.

comments.author: @ATLAS_UNBOUND_26
comments.text: Switzerland is one of those places where even ordinary daily life looks like a painting. The fact that people just wake up every morning with the Alps outside their window still blows my mind.

replies.author: @RemoteEarthJourneys
replies.text: That's exactly what amazes me too. For most of us, views like that are a dream vacation, while for many Swiss people it's just another Tuesday morning. Do you think you'd ever get used to scenery like that?

replies.text: If I could, I would move to the Alps in a heartbeat.

comments.author: @TopUnrealPlanet4K
comments.text: This is the kind of video that reminds you why exploration content can be so powerful when it’s done right. The visuals are stunning, but it’s the emotional storytelling and sense of mystery that truly elevate it into something unforgettable. It slowly pulls you into another world and leaves you thinking about it long after it ends. If that same sense of curiosity is still lingering for anyone else here, we just released a video that continues in that same direction but uncovers even more shocking and fascinating secrets.

replies.author: @RemoteEarthJourneys
replies.text: I agree that the storytelling is what makes the difference. Beautiful landscapes can grab your attention, but it's the history, mystery, and emotions behind them that make a place stay in your mind long after the video ends.

replies.author: @BestTravel2
replies.text: Wow, thank you so much for such a thoughtful and insightful comment! It truly means a lot to me that you connected with the emotional storytelling and sense of mystery. Your words really affirm why I do what I do.
